era_context: The year 1397 falls in the late 14th century, a period of significant political and cultural transition in Europe, Asia, and the Middle East. In Europe, the late Medieval period was characterized by feudal fragmentation, dynastic conflicts, and the early stirrings of the Renaissance in Italy. In Asia, the Ming Dynasty was consolidating its rule in China, the Timurid Empire was at its height in Central Asia and Persia, and the Ottoman Empire was expanding in Anatolia and the Balkans. This is an era of cultural flourishing, military conflict, and dynastic transitions.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Southeast Asian kingdoms (sources exist but underrepresented)
Mesoamerica and South America (sparse sources for this period)

events.title: Kalmar Union Established (Scandinavia)
events.description: The Kalmar Union was established in 1397 when the three Scandinavian kingdoms of Denmark, Norway, and Sweden were united under a single monarch, Margaret I of Denmark. The union was formalized at a meeting in Kalmar, Sweden, and Margaret I was recognized as the sovereign of all three kingdoms. The Kalmar Union lasted until 1523 and represented a significant political consolidation in Northern Europe. The union was designed to strengthen the Scandinavian kingdoms against the Hanseatic League and other external threats. Margaret I, known as 'the Lady King,' was one of the most powerful female rulers of medieval Europe.
key_figures: Margaret I of Denmark (Queen of Denmark, Norway, and Sweden)
Eric of Pomerania (Margaret's great-nephew, designated heir)

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on the Kalmar Union

events.certainty_note: The establishment of the Kalmar Union in 1397 is well-documented in Scandinavian sources. The date and circumstances are firmly established.
cross_references: Contemporary with other European dynastic consolidations

events.title: Hongwu Emperor Rules Ming China (Final Year)
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang), founder of the Ming Dynasty, was in the final year of his reign in 1397. He had ruled for 29 years (1368–1398) and had established the Ming Dynasty as one of the most powerful and culturally significant dynasties in Chinese history. The Hongwu Emperor was known for his authoritarian rule, his efforts to consolidate Ming power, and his cultural patronage. He would die in June 1398, and his son, the Jianwen Emperor, would ascend to the throne. The year 1397 marks the final phase of the Hongwu Emperor's reign.
key_figures: Hongwu Emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang; Ming Dynasty founder, r. 1368–1398)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Hongwu Emperor's rule in 1397 is well-documented in the Ming Shilu and other contemporary sources.

events.title: Timur Consolidates Timurid Empire (Central Asia)
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane), the Central Asian conqueror, was consolidating his vast empire in 1397. He had conquered territories stretching from Anatolia to India and was in his late 70s. By 1397, Timur was planning his final major campaign—an invasion of China—which would begin in 1405 but would be cut short by his death in February 1405. The year 1397 marks a period of consolidation and planning in the Timurid Empire as Timur prepared for his last great conquest.
events.certainty_note: Timur's consolidation of the Timurid Empire in 1397 is documented in later Timurid chronicles. The exact chronology of his final years is somewhat uncertain.

events.title: Ottoman Empire Expands Under Bayezid I
events.description: Bayezid I (r. 1389–1402), known as 'Yildirim' (the Thunderbolt), was ruling the Ottoman Empire in 1397 and continuing to expand Ottoman territory in Anatolia and the Balkans. By 1397, the Ottoman Empire had become one of the major powers in the eastern Mediterranean and was posing an increasing threat to the Byzantine Empire. Bayezid I's reign marked a period of Ottoman military success and territorial expansion that would continue until his defeat by Timur in 1402.
events.certainty_note: Bayezid I's rule of the Ottoman Empire in 1397 is well-documented in Ottoman and Byzantine sources.
cross_references: Leads to 1402-002 (Bayezid I defeated by Timur)

events.title: Richard II Rules England (Late Medieval Period)
events.description: Richard II, King of England, was ruling in 1397 in the penultimate year before his deposition. He had ruled since 1377 and was known for his autocratic style, artistic patronage, and unpopular policies. By 1397, Richard was facing increasing opposition from English nobles dissatisfied with his rule. His cousin, Henry Bolingbroke, Duke of Lancaster, was in exile and would return in 1399 to depose Richard. The year 1397 marks a period of political tension in England as Richard's rule became increasingly unpopular.
key_figures: Henry Bolingbroke (Duke of Lancaster, later Henry IV)

sources.name: English royal records and parliamentary documents

events.certainty_note: Richard II's rule in 1397 is well-documented in contemporary English sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The Kalmar Union (1397-001) is sometimes presented as a straightforward political consolidation, but modern scholarship emphasizes that it was a personal union under Margaret I and that the three kingdoms retained significant autonomy. The union was not as centralized as later nation-states, and tensions between the kingdoms would persist throughout the union's existence. The Hongwu Emperor's rule (1397-002) is sometimes presented as a period of stability, but his authoritarian policies and succession disputes suggest that the Ming Dynasty was facing internal challenges. Timur's consolidation of the Timurid Empire (1397-003) is sometimes presented as a straightforward process, but the empire was already showing signs of fragmentation as Timur's health declined.
historiographic_note: The year 1397 is moderately well-documented in European, Islamic, and Chinese sources. European sources (English, Scandinavian, Italian, Portuguese chronicles) are abundant. Islamic sources (Ottoman and Timurid chronicles) are available but often written decades after events. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) are contemporary and detailed. However, sources from sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are sparse or absent. The surviving sources reflect the interests of literate elites and likely underrepresent the experiences of common people, women, and non-elite groups. The historiography of 1397 is also shaped by the later significance of events that began in this year (e.g., the Kalmar Union, the final years of Richard II's reign), which may lead to retrospective overemphasis on their importance at the time.

graph_edges.note: The Kalmar Union established in 1397 lasted until 1523 when Sweden withdrew
The Hongwu Emperor's final year in 1397 led to his death and succession in 1398
Timur's consolidation of the Timurid Empire in 1397 led to his death and empire fragmentation in 1405
Bayezid I's expansion of the Ottoman Empire in 1397 led to his defeat by Timur in 1402
Richard II's unpopular rule in 1397 led to his deposition in 1399
